items.title: Rotterdam criteria
items.contentHtml:
Rotterdam Criteria

  • Diagnosis requires at least 2 of 3 criteria: oligoanovulation, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism and micropolycystic ovarian morphology on ultrasound.
  • Exclusion of other disorders (non-classical congenital adrenal hyperplasia, Cushing's syndrome, secretory tumors of androgens, hyperprolactinemia) is essential before confirming polycystic ovary syndrome.
  • The Rotterdam criteria are the most internationally accepted; they do not require all three for diagnosis, unlike the National Institutes of Health criteria (which require anovulation and hyperandrogenism).

items.title: Clinical and biochemical hyperandrogenism
items.contentHtml:
Clinical and biochemical hyperandrogenism

  • Clinical hyperandrogenism includes: hirsutism (Ferriman-Gallwey score >8), acne adult pattern, androgenic alopecia (male pattern), and seborrhea.
  • The Biochemical hyperandrogenism is confirmed with elevation of total or free testosterone, androstenedione or dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate; the measurement of free testosterone is more sensitive than the total one.
  • Trap: biochemical hyperandrogenism may be absent in up to 20-30% of patients with the ovulatory phenotype; The isolated symptoms do not rule out the diagnosis if there are other criteria.

items.title: Chronic anovulation and oligomenorrhea
items.contentHtml:
Chronic anovulation and oligomenorrhea

  • oligomenorrhea is defined as menstrual cycles with an interval of >35 days or <8 cycles per year; amenorrhea (>90 days without menstruation) indicates severe anovulation.
  • Chronic anovulation in polycystic ovary syndrome is a consequence of the alteration in the pulsatility of luteinizing hormone and insulin resistance, which prevent follicular maturation.
  • Abnormal uterine bleeding due to prolonged anovulation (>12 months) increases the risk of endometrial hyperplasia and endometrial cancer; Endometrial evaluation is required if there is persistent irregular bleeding or additional risk factors.

items.title: Ultrasound: micropolycystic ovarian morphology
items.contentHtml:
Ultrasound: micropolycystic ovarian morphology

  • Transvaginal ultrasound is the standard; The current criterion (2018 consensus) is the presence of >20 follicles per ovary 2-9 mm in diameter, or an ovarian volume >10 mL (in the absence of a dominant follicle >10 mm or corpus luteum).
  • [Key finding]: The “string of pearls” arrangement is typical, but not necessary for diagnosis; Measurement of ovarian volume is more reproducible than follicular counting.
  • In adolescents, ultrasound is not mandatory for diagnosis if there is hyperandrogenism and oligoanovulation, due to the frequency of physiological multifollicular ovaries.

items.title: Insulin resistance and hyperinsulinemia
items.contentHtml:
Insulin resistance and hyperinsulinemia

  • Insulin resistance is present in 50-70% of women with polycystic ovary syndrome, regardless of body mass index, and is a key factor in the pathophysiology of hyperandrogenism.
  • Compensatory hyperinsulinemia stimulates ovarian androgen production by increasing the sensitivity of theca cells to luteinizing hormone and by inhibiting hepatic synthesis of sex hormone binding globulin.
  • The evaluation of insulin resistance is performed using the HOMA-IR index (fasting glucose [mmol/L] × fasting insulin [mU/L] / 22.5); a value >2.5 suggests significant resistance, although it is not a formal diagnostic criterion.

items.title: Increased risk of type 2 diabetes
items.contentHtml:
Increased risk of type 2 diabetes

  • Women with polycystic ovary syndrome have a 3 to 7 times increased risk of developing type 2 diabetes mellitus compared to the general population, even adjusting for body mass index.
  • Screening for type 2 diabetes should be performed with 75 g oral glucose tolerance test (not only fasting glucose) starting at age 40 or earlier if there are risk factors (body mass index >25 kg/m², family history, metabolic syndrome).
  • Glucose intolerance (2-hour glucose between 140 and 199 mg/dL) is common and should be treated aggressively with lifestyle changes and metformin to prevent progression to diabetes.

items.title: First-line treatment: metformin
items.contentHtml:
First-line treatment: metformin

  • Metformin is the first-line treatment for insulin resistance and prevention of type 2 diabetes in women with polycystic ovary syndrome, especially in those with high body mass index or glucose intolerance.
  • The starting dose is 500 mg every 12 days. hours with food, increasing to 850 mg every 12 hours or 1000 mg every 12 hours as tolerated; the maximum dose is 2550 mg/day.
  • Metformin is not a first-line ovulation inducer, but it improves spontaneous ovulation rates and response to clomiphene citrate; Its most common adverse effects are gastrointestinal (diarrhea, nausea).

items.title: Combined oral contraceptives as management
items.contentHtml:
Combined oral contraceptives as management

  • Combined oral contraceptives (ethinyl estradiol + progestogen) are the first-line treatment for clinical hyperandrogenism (hirsutism, acne) and to regulate the menstrual cycle in women who do not want pregnancy.
  • The estrogenic component increases sex hormone binding globulin, reducing testosterone. free; progestogens with antiandrogenic effect (cyproterone, drospirenone) are preferred in cases of severe hyperandrogenism.
  • Contraindications: risk of venous thromboembolism, migraine with aura, uncontrolled hypertension, smoking in people over 35 years of age, active liver disease; The cardiovascular risk profile should be evaluated before starting.

items.title: Ovulation induction with clomiphene citrate
items.contentHtml:
Ovulation induction with clomiphene citrate

  • Clomiphene citrate is the first-line drug for ovulation induction in women with polycystic ovary syndrome who desire pregnancy and have anovulation.
  • The initial dose is 50 mg/day for 5 days, starting on day 3-5 of the cycle; if there is no ovulation, it is increased to 100 mg/day in the next cycle, and then up to 150 mg/day (maximum dose).
  • The ovulation rate reaches 70-80%, but the pregnancy rate is lower (30-40%) due to possible antiestrogenic effects on the endometrium and cervical mucus; the risk of multiple pregnancy is 5-10%.

items.title: Associated metabolic syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Common associated metabolic syndrome

  • The metabolic syndrome is defined by the presence of at least 3 of 5 criteria: waist circumference >88 cm, triglycerides >150 mg/dL, high-density lipoprotein cholesterol <50 mg/dL, blood pressure >130/85 mmHg and fasting glucose >100 mg/dL.
  • In polycystic ovary syndrome, the prevalence of metabolic syndrome is 30-50%, especially in women with high body mass index and insulin resistance.
  • Comprehensive management includes lifestyle changes (low-calorie diet, exercise aerobic), metformin and, if the alterations persist, specific treatment for each component (statins, antihypertensives).

items.title: Screening for obstructive sleep apnea
items.contentHtml:
Screening for obstructive sleep apnea

  • Women with polycystic ovary syndrome have a 5 to 10 times increased risk of developing obstructive sleep apnea, regardless of body mass index, due to insulin resistance and hyperandrogenism.
  • Screening is recommended in patients with obesity (body mass index >30 kg/m²), snoring, excessive daytime sleepiness or resistant hypertension; the STOP-Bang questionnaire is used (score ≥3 suggests high risk).
  • Confirmatory diagnosis requires polysomnography; The first-line treatment is continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP), which improves insulin sensitivity and metabolic profile.

items.foco: Risk of endometrial hyperplasia due to anovulation
items.title: Risk of endometrial hyperplasia
items.contentHtml:
Risk of endometrial hyperplasia due to anovulation

  • Chronic anovulation without progesterone opposition exposes the endometrium to continuous estrogen stimulation, which increases the risk of endometrial hyperplasia (relative risk 2-4 times) and endometrial cancer.
  • Evaluation is recommended endometrial (transvaginal ultrasound with measurement of endometrial thickness and biopsy if >5 mm or irregular bleeding) in women with prolonged anovulation (>12 months) or additional risk factors (obesity, diabetes, age >35 years).
  • Endometrial protection is achieved with combined oral contraceptives or cyclic progestogens (medroxyprogesterone 10 mg/day for 12-14 days each month) in women who do not want pregnancy; In those seeking fertility, ovulation induction also reduces the risk.
